title: CAREERS Act
plain_summary: The CAREERS Act updates a government program that helps create jobs in rural areas. The program gives money to organizations that train workers for careers in important industries like healthcare, manufacturing, and utilities. The law makes it easier for different types of groups to get funding and requires them to focus on helping workers in rural communities where many young people are moving away. Organizations that receive money must report on how well their training programs are working to help people find good jobs.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Creating Access to Rural Employment and Education for Resilience and Success Act or the CAREERS Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Workforce Training Programs
sections.text: This section changes an existing law about helping rural communities with job training and economic development. The changes add new types of schools and organizations that can help with these programs. These include career and technical education schools and local workforce development boards that help people find jobs in their area. The law now includes career pathway programs, which are organized series of education and training activities that help people get better jobs. It also includes industry or sector partnerships, which are groups of employers and other organizations working together to address workforce needs in specific industries. The updated law allows grants to be used for career pathway programs and industry partnerships in rural communities. These programs must help with workforce challenges, promote skills development, encourage innovation, and boost economic growth in rural areas. Grant applications must show how they will address problems like worker displacement when jobs are lost, an aging workforce, and young people leaving rural areas. The Secretary of Agriculture must make sure grant money is distributed to different regions across the country. Grants can now support career pathway programs and industry partnerships in many different industries important to rural communities. These include telecommunications and internet services, water and waste services, electric services, environmental conservation, health care, child care, manufacturing, and agricultural businesses involved in producing, processing, and distributing food and farm products. Other industries can be included if local workforce boards identify them as having many job openings. Organizations that receive grants must report on how many people completed skills training, earned recognized credentials, or gained specialized education through these programs. They must also report on employment and earnings outcomes for people who participated. The law extends funding authorization from two thousand nineteen through two thousand twenty three to two thousand twenty five through two thousand thirty. These changes will take effect on a date chosen by the Secretary of Agriculture, but no later than one year after this law is passed.
